node.html: Repurposed an old laptop into an always-on, containerized homelab: an AI agent (Hermes) running as a persistent service with Telegram integration and Docker-sandboxed command execution, a Cloudflare Tunnel + Access setup exposing this site with zero inbound ports opened, and GNOME Remote Desktop reachable both over LAN and externally through the same tunnel. Every service runs in its own Docker Compose stack with explicit memory/CPU limits, since the whole box runs on ~5.7GB of RAM.

frontmatter.title: Visual Emotion Detection — Model Survey & Evaluation

node.html: Benchmarking lightweight CV/FER models (DeepFace, HSEmotion, EfficientFace, mini-xception) against vision-language models (Moondream2, Qwen2.5-VL) for real-time visual emotion detection under a 6GB VRAM budget — built to responsibly pick a model that feeds a student's live emotional state into an LLM tutoring session. Every candidate is scored on the same 150-image FER2013 sample under an explicit capability/license/VRAM/latency/accuracy exclusion order, rather than comparing vendor-quoted numbers.

A weighted-voting ensemble (Lingua, OpenLID, pycld2, fastText, langdetect) built to fix short-text language detection for English/Malay/Indonesian, where the production baseline (langdetect alone) scored 29.1% overall accuracy and couldn't detect Malay at all. The best ensemble configuration reaches 70.8% overall accuracy — a +41.7pp improvement — while running 8.6-115.5x faster per call, evaluated across 1,273 real EN/MY/ID short-text cases bucketed by word count.

Comparative benchmark of 10 open-source TTS models for Malay, Indonesian, and English zero-shot voice cloning, all actually installed and run (or documented as blocked) on the same RTX 3050 6GB hardware and scored on WER, speaker similarity, VRAM, and prosody — rather than relying on marketing claims about language support. Selected VoxCPM2 as the production candidate: the only model with native Malay support, and one of only two with any Indonesian support at all.

Deployable emotion-recognition service wrapping a compact CNN (EfficientNet-B0, ~4M params) behind a local API, returning 8 emotion probabilities plus valence/arousal from a cropped face. Built with two interchangeable inference backends (ONNX Runtime and OpenVINO) verified to ~1e-6 relative parity against each other, and two deployment modes sharing one contract: a server-side FastAPI endpoint, and a client-side build running the same model directly in the browser via onnxruntime-web — verified byte-identical output between the two. Diagnosed and fixed a real production bug where sending whole webcam frames (instead of cropped faces) silently collapsed every prediction to " neutral." Try the live client-side demo above, next to my name — it runs the same model, in your browser, right now. When a session ends, the browser sends a small summary (no video/images) to a companion FastAPI service at emotion-api.paksopi.me, which does no inference of its own — just receives and displays it, the same client/server split shown here.
Agricultural machinery rental platform built and hosted with real-time availability logic, letting farmers browse and book equipment as it actually becomes free rather than against a static schedule.
Futsal court booking system delivered as part of a Scrum team, coordinating sprint planning and delivery with teammates to ship a working reservation platform on a cPanel-hosted PHP/MySQL stack.
